title: Congenital heart disease - NHS
text:
r during the 20-week pregnancy screening scan . The 20-week scan cannot find all conditions, so it’s possible for congenital heart disease to be missed.
Sometimes it is not diagnosed until you’re an adult.
Tests for congenital heart disease in children and adults include:
measuring how much oxygen is in your blood (pulse oximetry test) measuring the electrical activity of the heart ( electrocardiogram ) chest X-rays scans to check your heart and the surrounding blood vessels, such as an MRI and echocardiogram blood tests to check for genetic alterations (mutated genes) that may cause heart problems studying the flow of blood through your heart using a special dye that shows up on scanners ( cardiac catheterisation and coronary angiography ) 
Types of congenital heart disease 
There are many different types of congenital heart disease and how it is treated will vary depending on the type.
Some types can have an immediate effect on the body and need surgery soon after birth.
Other types, such as a hole in the heart can often be left untreated.
Information: 
Find out more Understanding your congenital heart condition (British Heart Foundation) Congenital heart disease (CHD): information for parents (GOV.UK) 
Treatment for congenital heart disease 
If you or your child are diagnosed with congenital heart disease, you’ll be referred to a specialist team at an NHS hospital.
